subject: Chemistry
title: Chlorine Trifluoride: Burns Anything
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Chlorine Trifluoride: Burns Anything.

hook.text: Chlorine trifluoride is so reactive it sets fire to glass, sand, water, and even asbestos.

buildup.text: It's a stronger oxidizer than oxygen itself. Materials that can't normally burn will ignite on contact.

discovery.text: Nazis tried to use it as a weapon. It was too dangerous—it corroded its own storage containers.

twist.text: A spill at a factory once burned through 30 cm of concrete and a meter of gravel beneath it.

climax.text: It's still used in tiny amounts to clean semiconductor equipment. Handle with extreme caution.

punchline.text: Burns glass, concrete, and water. Chemistry's most terrifying liquid.

quiz.question: What makes chlorine trifluoride so dangerous?
options: It is a stronger oxidizer than oxygen and ignites almost any material
It explodes when exposed to air
